1: The oracle against Babylon opens with the cryptic place-name Leb-qamai — a cipher (atbash code, reversing the Hebrew alphabet) for Kasdim (Chaldea/Babylon). The same cryptic substitution is used for "Sheshach" = Babel (Jeremiah 25:26; Jeremiah 51:41). The use of coded language may reflect the political danger of openly declaring Babylon's fall while Babylonian power was at its peak, or it may be a literary device that signals the insider knowledge of the prophetic tradition. YHWH stirs up a destroying wind (ruaḥ mashḥit) against Babylon — the wind of cosmic destruction that recalls the creation narrative of Genesis 1.
2: The winnowing wind — sent to winnow Babylon and empty its land — extends the agricultural metaphor of separation. Winnowing used wind to separate grain from chaff; the destroyers sent against Babylon will perform the cosmic separation of her people from the land. The emptying of her land on the day of disaster echoes the language of the Exodus plagues, when Egypt was emptied. Babylon is being subjected to an Exodus-like judgment, consistent with the book's repeated use of Exodus typology for the fall of the great oppressor.
3: The military instruction — let the archer bend his bow against her — addresses the attacking forces directly. The irony of the command is that the archer is invited to do what seems impossible: attack the most powerful military state of the ancient world. The phrase do not spare her young men, devote her entire army to destruction uses the ḥerem vocabulary (devote to destruction) that the Torah applied to Canaanite cities (Deuteronomy 7:2; Jeremiah 20:17). Babylon is to be treated as the object of total sacred destruction — the same fate Israel was commanded to execute on Canaan.
4: The result — the slain will fall in the land of the Chaldeans, those thrust through in her streets — describes the defeat of Babylon's army in her own homeland and cities. The Babylonian army, which had devastated the streets of Jerusalem and left bodies throughout Judean cities, will now face the same fate in their own streets. The reversal of fortune is precise: what Babylon did to Israel will be done to Babylon. The covenant principle of measure-for-measure operates across the OAN.
5: The declaration that Israel and Judah have not been forsaken by their God interrupts the judgment oracle against Babylon with a covenant affirmation. Despite the exile, YHWH has not dissolved the covenant relationship. The land of the Chaldeans is full of guilt before the Holy One of Israel — the divine title Qedosh Yisraʾel (Holy One of Israel), characteristic of Isaiah, appears here to identify YHWH's holiness as the basis for both Israel's preservation and Babylon's judgment. YHWH's holiness cannot permanently tolerate the oppressor of his people.
6: The call to flee from Babylon, everyone save yourselves — do not perish in her punishment — is addressed to the exile community in Babylon. The three warnings to flee (here, Jeremiah 51:45, and Jeremiah 51:50) frame the oracle as genuine advance warning: the community has time to escape before the judgment falls. This evacuation oracle is directly cited in Revelation 18:4 ("come out of her, my people"), making Babylon a permanent symbol of the system from which God's people must separate themselves before divine judgment falls.
7: Babylon was a golden cup in YHWH's hand, making all the earth drunk — the cup of divine wrath that Jeremiah described in Jeremiah 25:15-17 (given to all nations to drink) is now identified as Babylon herself. She was YHWH's instrument for dispensing judgment to the nations; the nations drank from her and went mad. The golden cup image presents Babylon not merely as a political empire but as a divine instrument of global judgment — which makes her own subsequent judgment all the more dramatic. The instrument of wrath is itself subjected to wrath.
8: Suddenly Babylon has fallen and been broken — the adverb pitʾom (suddenly) is theologically important: Babylon's fall comes without warning, demonstrating that it was not the product of human planning but of divine decree. The call to wail for her, bring balm for her pain — perhaps she can be healed — contains an ironic echo of Jeremiah 46:11 (where Egypt was told to go to Gilead for balm): there is no healing for Babylon either. The ʾulay tirape (perhaps she can be healed) is deliberately ironic; her destruction is decreed.
9: The nations' response — we tried to heal Babylon but she could not be healed; leave her, let each of us go to our own land — imagines the subject nations abandoning their attempts to stabilize the fallen empire. The phrase for her judgment reaches to the heavens, it is lifted up even to the skies signals that the divine verdict against Babylon is cosmic in scale — it has reached heaven, where YHWH sits enthroned as judge. What is decreed in heaven cannot be reversed by earthly intervention.
10: The covenant community's response — YHWH has vindicated us; come let us declare in Zion the work of YHWH our God — frames the fall of Babylon as covenant vindication. The word tsidqotenu (our righteousnesses/vindications) means that YHWH has demonstrated the justice of his covenant by punishing the oppressor. The declaration of YHWH's acts in Zion — the holy city — completes the reversal: from Babylon's cup dispensing judgment to Zion proclaiming YHWH's vindication. Proclamation in Zion had been silenced by the exile; now it resumes.
11: The military command — sharpen the arrows, fill the quivers — addressed to the armies attacking Babylon uses specific military equipment terminology. The identification of the attackers as the kings of the Medes (malkei Madai) is a precise historical reference: it was Cyrus the Persian (with Median allies) who conquered Babylon in 539 BC. The oracle identifies Babylon's conquerors by ethnic name, a specificity comparable to Isaiah's naming of Cyrus (Isaiah 44:28; Jeremiah 45:1). The historical accuracy of this identification has been significant in debates about the prophetic tradition.
12: The signal raised against the walls of Babylon — the posting of sentries, preparing ambushes — reflects standard siege warfare tactics. That YHWH both planned and carried out his declaration against Babylon frames the siege as the execution of a divine decree rather than a military operation. The vocabulary of YHWH planning (zamam) and doing (ʿasah) what he declared (dibber) concerning Babylon's inhabitants is the prophetic claim that history implements divine speech.
13: The description of Babylon as dwelling beside many waters, abundant in treasures accurately captures ancient Babylon's geography: situated between the Euphrates and Tigris rivers with an extensive canal network, it was the commercial hub of the ancient Near East. The Euphrates actually ran through the city; the canal system (documented in Neo-Babylonian administrative texts) supported both agriculture and trade. The end of your thread of life has come, the measure of your plunder — the image of a thread (amma) that is cut signals the divine termination of Babylon's allotted span.
14: YHWH's oath by himself — I have sworn by myself (nishbaʿ YHWH bifshô) — is the strongest possible divine commitment (cf. Genesis 22:16; Isaiah 45:23; Amos 6:8). He has filled Babylon with enemies like a swarm of locusts — the locust image for a numerous army appears in Nahum 3:15-17 and Joel 1-2, each time conveying irresistible numerical superiority. The enemy swarm will raise a victory shout over Babylon, ending her millennial dominance.
15: The creation hymn — he made the earth by his power, established the world by his wisdom, and stretched out the heavens by his understanding — is identical to 10:12 and appears to be a liturgical fragment deployed here as a counter-claim to Babylonian cosmology. The Babylonian creation story (Enuma Elish) attributed creation to Marduk's victory over Tiamat; Jeremiah's creation hymn attributes creation to YHWH's wisdom and power. Against Babylon's cosmological propaganda, this hymn asserts YHWH's creative sovereignty as the basis for his ability to judge Babylon.
16: The phenomena — when he thunders, the waters in the heavens roar; he causes the vapors to rise from the ends of the earth, he makes lightning for the rain and brings out the wind from his storehouses — attribute all meteorological phenomena to YHWH's direct control. In ANE religious thought, storm, rain, and thunder were the domain of the storm deity (Baal, Hadad, Adad, Marduk). YHWH's claim over these phenomena is a direct polemic against the entire Babylonian and Canaanite divine hierarchy. The one who controls storms controls history.
17: The idol polemic (identical to Jeremiah 10:14-15) — every human is stupid and without knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his idols, for his cast images are a fraud and there is no breath in them — deployed in the middle of the Babylon oracle frames Babylon's defeat as the logical consequence of idol worship. Babylon's gods (Marduk, Bel, Nebo, Tiamat) are empty objects; their worshippers are consequently without wisdom (daʿat). The nation that trusts in non-gods cannot stand before the living God.
18: The description of the idols as worthless, objects of mockery at the time of their judgment — the same language used in Jeremiah 10:15 — frames the Babylon oracle within the idol-polemic tradition. When Babylon falls, her gods will be exposed as fraudulent; the judgment is simultaneously political and theological. The gods of Babylon will be publicly demonstrated to be nothing, just as the absence of divine protection was read in ANE thought as proof of divine failure.
19: The contrast — the Portion of Jacob is not like these, for he is the former of all things, and Israel is the tribe of his inheritance, YHWH of hosts is his name — closes the idol-polemic by identifying YHWH as the maker of all and Israel as his possession. The title ḥeleq Yaʿaqov (Portion of Jacob) frames YHWH as Israel's allocated share — just as Israel is YHWH's portion. This mutual belonging (ḥeleq language appears in Numbers 18:20 for the Levites) grounds both the judgment of Babylon and the salvation of Israel in the covenant relationship.
20: You are my war club and weapon of war; with you I shatter nations, with you I destroy kingdoms — YHWH addresses Babylon as his instrument in a series of nine pairs of shattering. The "war club" (maphets) is the battle-mace, a weapon for crushing — in Egyptian and Mesopotamian royal iconography, the pharaoh or king is depicted striking enemies with a mace. YHWH has wielded Babylon as his mace against the nations. This continues the theology of Jeremiah 25:9 (Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH's servant), now extended to its full martial imagery.
21: The shattering extends through every social category: horse and rider, chariot and charioteer — military power; man and woman — civilian population; old man and youth — every generation. Each pair represents a complete social category; nine pairs (Jeremiah 51:20-23) suggest comprehensive cosmic scope. The use of Babylon as the divine war club is the OAN's most direct statement of Babylon's function as YHWH's instrument — and simultaneously sets up the reversal: having been YHWH's instrument, Babylon will now become the target of YHWH's instrument.
22: The shattering of shepherd and flock, farmer and team, governors and prefects extends the pairs through both pastoral and administrative categories. The inclusion of governors (peḥot) and prefects (seganim) — administrative terms for provincial officials appearing throughout the exilic period — suggests the oracle addresses the complex bureaucratic structure of the Babylonian empire. The entire administrative apparatus, through which Babylon exercised control over subject peoples including Judah, will be shattered by YHWH's hand.
23: The conclusion of the war-club oracle — I will repay Babylon and all the inhabitants of Chaldea for all the evil they have done in Zion before your eyes, declares YHWH — pivots from instrument to object of judgment. Having completed the shattering mission for which YHWH deployed Babylon, YHWH now turns to repay Babylon for the specific evil done in Zion. The witness formula leʿeinekem (before your eyes) addresses the exile community in Babylon: they will see Babylon repaid for the destruction of their homeland they witnessed.
24: The public and visible nature of the repayment — I will repay Babylon and all the inhabitants of Chaldea for all the evil they did in Zion before your eyes — makes the exile community both witnesses and beneficiaries of the judgment. The destruction of Jerusalem was witnessed by those watching in horror; the fall of Babylon will be witnessed by those watching in vindication. Prophetic promises find their intended audience in the people who suffered under the condemned power.
25: The declaration against the destroying mountain (har hammashḥit) — Babylon — that destroys the whole earth: YHWH will stretch out his hand against her, roll her from the crags, and make her a burned-out mountain. The image of a destroyed mountain that is no longer suitable for building is powerful: mountains in ANE symbolism were cosmic centers, seats of divine power (Zaphon = YHWH's mountain, Babylon's ziggurat as artificial mountain). A burned mountain — ash, unusable stone — is the anti-cosmic image of Babylon stripped of all sacred significance.
26: The declaration that no stone will be taken from Babylon for a corner, no stone for a foundation — the destroyed city's stones will be unusable — signals permanent desolation. In antiquity, conquered cities were often demolished but their stones reused in new construction; the decree that Babylon's stones cannot even serve as building material is the ultimate verdict of permanent worthlessness. Archaeologically, ancient Babylon's ruins (at Hillah, Iraq) were extensively plundered for building material in later periods, yet the core was never rebuilt as a habited city.
27: The signal to the nations — lift up a banner in the land; blow the trumpet among the nations; consecrate nations against her; summon against her the kingdoms of Ararat, Minni, and Ashkenaz — names three kingdoms of the Iranian/Armenian highlands. Ararat (Urartu, around Lake Van in modern eastern Turkey), Minni (Mannaeans, southeast of Lake Urmia), and Ashkenaz (Scythians, possibly in the northern steppes) are all peoples who participated in the coalition that defeated the Neo-Babylonian empire. The Nabonidus Chronicle describes the Medes and Persians as the primary agents; these peoples were part of the broader alliance.
28: The consecrating of nations for war against Babylon — consecrate against her the kingdoms of Media, its governors and all its officials — uses the ḥerem/sacred war vocabulary. The kings of the Medes and their officials and their whole domain will march against Babylon. Cyrus the Great (who conquered Babylon in 539 BC) was the Achaemenid Persian king who had absorbed the Median empire; the oracle's reference to the Medes reflects the Median component of the Persian coalition, consistent with how ANE sources describe Cyrus's forces.
29: The trembling and writhing of the earth — the land trembles and writhes in agony — at YHWH's purpose against Babylon renders the judgment a cosmic event, not merely a military operation. The earth itself participates in the response to divine decree. The purpose (maḥshevot YHWH, YHWH's plans) to make Babylon a desolation without inhabitant is fulfilled when even the cosmic order participates in the judgment. The land (ʾerets) can mean both the earth generally and the specific land of Babylon.
30: The description of Babylon's warriors ceasing to fight — the warriors of Babylon have stopped fighting; they sit in their strongholds; their strength has failed; they have become women — uses the cultural idiom of warriors reduced to female passivity as the image of total defeat. Historical accounts of Babylon's fall in 539 BC (Cyrus Cylinder, Nabonidus Chronicle) describe a relatively peaceful surrender: Babylon's gates were opened without major combat, and Cyrus entered without destruction. The oracle's image of warriors ceasing to fight matches this historical tradition of a defeat achieved through demoralization rather than battle.
31: The cascade of messengers — one runner runs to meet another, one messenger meets another — conveys the rapid collapse of the empire through the image of news spreading from the periphery to the center. The runners bringing news to the king of Babylon that his city is captured from end to end, the fords have been seized, the reed marshes set on fire, the soldiers are terrified — describe the systematic conquest of the Babylonian heartland. The reed marshes of the Euphrates delta (southern Mesopotamia) were strategic military terrain and agricultural resource.
32: The seizure of the fords (hamaʿbarot) was militarily critical: the Euphrates crossings controlled access to and from the city. Ancient sources report that Cyrus diverted the Euphrates to lower the water level in the channel beneath Babylon's walls, allowing troops to enter through the riverbed — a dramatic example of the "fords seized" that this verse anticipates. The combination of seized crossings, burning marshes, and terrified soldiers creates a picture of rapid, multi-front collapse.
33: The metaphor of Babylon as a threshing floor at the time it is trodden — yet a little while and the time of her harvest will come — takes the agricultural image of judgment (grain being threshed, chaff separated) and applies it to Babylon. The threshing floor was where final separations were made; harvest time was the moment of accounting. The phrase ʿod meʿat (yet a little while) signals imminent fulfillment — the harvest of judgment for Babylon is almost ready. This agricultural metaphor for divine judgment runs throughout the Hebrew prophets.
34: The voice of Jerusalem/Zion — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon has devoured me, has crushed me; he has swallowed me like a monster, he has filled his belly with my delicacies; he has driven me out — presents the city personified as a victim crying for justice. The language of being swallowed (belaʿani) by a tannin (sea-dragon/Leviathan) invokes mythological imagery: Babylon is the chaos monster that consumed YHWH's people. This cry from the victim to the divine judge constitutes the covenant lawsuit that grounds the subsequent judgment.
35: The further cry — may the violence done to me and my flesh be upon Babylon — is a formal demand for legal restitution. The phrase ḥamasi weʾsheri ʿal Bavel (my violence and my flesh upon Babylon) invokes the blood-guilt principle: the violence done to Jerusalem must be answered by violence upon the perpetrator. The call for YHWH to be the avenger (goʾel) brings the covenant kinsman-redeemer concept into play: YHWH who is Israel's nearest relative is obligated to avenge her blood.
36: YHWH's response — I will take up your cause and execute vengeance for you; I will dry up her sea and make her fountain dry — accepts the obligation of the divine redeemer. The drying of Babylon's sea and waters is both literal (the Euphrates and canal system that sustained the city) and symbolic (the primordial waters that Marduk organized in creation; YHWH will reverse Babylon's cosmogony). Babylon will be made a heap of ruins, a haunt of jackals — the standard formula for irreversible urban desolation.
37: The list of what Babylon will become — a heap of ruins, a haunt of jackals, an object of horror and hissing, without inhabitant — deploys the four-fold curse formula of permanent desolation used throughout Jeremiah (cf. Jeremiah 25:18; Jeremiah 44:22; Jeremiah 51:29). The jackal (tannim) is the characteristic animal of desolate ruins — its howl was the sound of complete urban abandonment. The archaeological reality of Babylon's ruins (Birs Nimrud, Tell es-Sahn) broadly confirms this oracle: a vast ruined site, never rebuilt as a major city.
38: The comparison of Babylon's warriors to young lions roaring and growling like lion cubs — well-fed, confident, fierce — sets up the reversal. The lion imagery for royal/military power is pervasive in ANE iconography (Babylonian lion-gate reliefs, the lion as symbol of the tribe of Judah, leonine guardian figures). YHWH will give them a feast — and then they will sleep forever. The reversal of the lion from predator to prey is the measure of Babylon's overthrow.
39: YHWH's ruse — when they are heated I will set out their feast; I will make them drunk, that they may rejoice, then sleep a perpetual sleep and not wake up — uses the wine/drunkenness metaphor (cf. Jeremiah 25:15-17; Jeremiah 51:7) for divine judgment. The image of warriors feasting and being made drunk by their host so they can be killed in their sleep reflects the story of Belshazzar's feast (Daniel 5), where Babylon's last king feasted while Cyrus's army entered the city. Ancient sources (Herodotus) report that Babylon was captured during a festival when the inhabitants were feasting.
40: YHWH bringing Babylon's warriors down like lambs to slaughter, like rams and male goats — the lamb/ram imagery inverts the lion imagery of Jeremiah 51:38. The fierce lions become compliant sacrificial animals; the predators become the prey. The ḥerem (sacred destruction) vocabulary implicit in the sacrifice image frames Babylon's fall as a holy offering to YHWH — the same language used for Israel's enemies throughout the conquest tradition.
41: How Babylon is captured, how the praise of all the earth is taken — the first word is ʾeikh (how), the lament cry that opens both Lamentations (Lamentations 1:1) and the dirge over fallen cities. The description of Babylon as the praise of the whole earth (cf. "praise of the nations" in Lamentations 2:15 for Jerusalem) acknowledges Babylon's genuine cultural and architectural magnificence. Nebuchadnezzar's Babylon was one of the wonders of the ancient world: the city walls, the hanging gardens, the Ishtar Gate (now reconstructed in Berlin's Pergamon Museum) were objects of international admiration.
42: The sea has come up over Babylon; she has been covered by its tumultuous waves — the sea swallowing Babylon is the reversal of Marduk's cosmogony. In the Enuma Elish, Marduk defeated Tiamat (the primordial sea-dragon) and used her body to create the heavens and earth, establishing Babylon as the cosmic center. Now the sea overcomes Babylon — YHWH undoes the Babylonian creation order, returning the empire to the primordial chaos over which Marduk claimed victory. The theological polemic is precise and devastating.
43: The cities of Babylon's territory — her cities have become a horror, a parched and desert land, a land where no one lives, no human passes through — describe the complete depopulation of the Babylonian heartland. The three descriptors (horror/desolation, parched land, uninhabited land) constitute a three-fold curse formula. The southern Mesopotamian marshes (now in Iraq) and the Babylonian cities never fully recovered their ancient population levels after the Persian and subsequent conquests.
44: The punishment of Bel in Babylon — making him disgorge what he has swallowed — directly addresses the chief deity of the Babylonian pantheon. Bel (the title meaning "Lord") was Marduk's primary title; the Bel temple (Esagila) in Babylon was the most important religious site in the empire. YHWH making Bel disgorge what he swallowed inverts the swallowing image of Jeremiah 51:34 (where Nebuchadnezzar swallowed Jerusalem): Bel/Babylon will be forced to regurgitate Israel. The nations that had streamed to Babylon will stream away; the walls of Babylon will fall.
45: The second evacuation call — come out of her, my people; save yourselves, each one, from YHWH's burning anger — repeats Jeremiah 51:6's urgent warning. The identification of the dangerous anger as specifically YHWH's anger (not merely Babylon's destruction) suggests that remaining in Babylon when YHWH's judgment falls means being caught in the collateral destruction of YHWH's wrath. The call ʿammi (my people) is the covenant address — YHWH speaks to his people embedded in the condemned empire.
46: The warning against faint hearts at the reports that reach you — the rumor of violence in the land, ruler against ruler — addresses the anxiety of the exile community hearing conflicting reports about Babylon's stability. Political instability in the empire (the Babylonian succession crises between Nebuchadnezzar's successors) would have generated rumors and anxiety. YHWH counsels against being demoralized by these reports: the coming judgment is real but arrives at the divine moment, not at every political disturbance.
47: The announcement of days coming when I will punish the images of Babylon — their idols will be put to shame — frames the conquest of Babylon as theological judgment against the Babylonian divine system. When Babylon falls, her gods are publicly demonstrated as powerless; the divine images that Babylon used as tools of ideological domination over subject peoples (including carrying captured divine images into Babylon) will be exposed as frauds. Cyrus's Cylinder proclaims that Marduk himself sent Cyrus to restore the divine images — the theological claim is contested even in the ancient sources.
48: The cosmic celebration — heaven and earth and all that is in them will shout for joy over Babylon, for the destroyers come against her from the north — transforms Babylon's fall into a cosmic liturgical event. The entire creation participates in the response to divine justice: heaven and earth join the chorus of vindication. The direction "from the north" (mitsapon) is again the directional indicator for the threat, this time applied to Babylon rather than Israel. For Babylon, the north brought the Medo-Persian forces; the directional reversal is complete.
49: The divine declaration — Babylon must fall for the slain of Israel, just as for Babylon the slain of all the earth have fallen — states the principle of proportional judgment with legal precision. Babylon killed the slain of all the earth; Babylon must now fall as payment. The covenant-lawsuit logic is explicit: the measure Babylon used will be measured back to her. The slain of Israel (ḥalele Yisraʾel) are specifically named, grounding the universal judgment in the particular covenant crime against YHWH's people.
50: The third evacuation call — you who have escaped the sword, go, do not stand still; remember YHWH from far away, and let Jerusalem come to your mind — addresses those who have already survived Babylon's judgment. The exiles are urged to remember Zion from their distant location and begin the mental and spiritual return that will precede the physical return. Zakar ʾet-YHWH (remember YHWH) is the covenant memory command that oriented Israel's entire life — festivals, Sabbath, Torah — toward the renewal of divine presence.
51: The community's confession of shame — we are put to shame, for we have heard reproach; disgrace has covered our faces, for foreigners have entered the holy places of YHWH's house — is the exile community's lament that the temple has been profaned. The entry of foreigners (zarim) into YHWH's house is the ultimate covenantal degradation: the sacred space where YHWH's presence dwelled has been desecrated. This shame motivates the longing for the restoration oracle that follows. The community's pain is the platform from which YHWH's vindication will be proclaimed.
52: YHWH's response — therefore behold, the days are coming when I will punish her idols and the wounded will groan throughout her land — reaffirms the judgment as future but certain. The connection between the exiles' shame and YHWH's punishment of Babylon's idols is the covenant lawsuit logic: the dishonor done to YHWH's house (by Babylon's soldiers defiling it) generates YHWH's dishonor of Babylon's gods (by having them defeated and their land ravaged). Honor requires honor; the divine king responds to the dishonoring of his sanctuary.
53: The declaration of the inescapability of judgment — though Babylon should mount up to heaven and though she should fortify her high stronghold, yet destroyers would come from me against her — invokes the Tower of Babel tradition (Genesis 11:1-9). Babylon's very name in the tradition was associated with the attempt to build a tower reaching heaven; that tower was confounded by YHWH. Now, even if Babylon should complete that ancient project and reach heaven itself, YHWH's destroyers would still reach her. No height of fortress or aspiration can escape divine judgment.
54: The sound of crying from Babylon — the sound of a cry from Babylon! Great destruction from the land of the Chaldeans! — uses auditory imagery to signal the fall of the empire from a distance. The sound of destruction (shattering, crashing, crying) cascades from the outer regions to the center. The call to YHWH to destroy and cut off from Babylon the great voice — the imperial voice that commanded nations — is the call to silence Babylon's dominance. When Babylon's great voice goes silent, the dominated nations are released.
55: YHWH's active role — for YHWH is laying Babylon waste and will silence her mighty voice, and their waves will roar like great waters, a noisy uproar will ring out — combines silence (the silencing of Babylon) with noise (the roar of the invading waters/armies). The contrast dramatizes the reversal: Babylon's voice goes silent while the sound of her destruction is deafening. The paradox of silence-within-noise is the experience of a city falling: the commanding voice of empire muffled by the roar of conquest.
56: The conqueror coming against Babylon — her warriors are captured, their bows broken, for YHWH is a God of recompense; he will fully repay — uses the broken bow as the symbol of military defeat (cf. Psalms 46:9; Hosea 1:5). A warrior's bow was his primary weapon and status symbol; its breaking signified complete disarmament and defeat. The theological declaration — YHWH is a God of recompense (El gemulot) — is a divine title asserting his nature as just repayer. Babylon's recompense for what she did to Israel is the structural principle of the entire oracle.
57: The divine oath — I will make her officials and her sages, her governors, her prefects and her warriors drunk; they will sleep a perpetual sleep and not wake up — repeats the wine/sleep/death judgment from Jeremiah 51:39. The comprehensive listing of Babylon's ruling class (officials, sages, governors, prefects, warriors) ensures no one in the Babylonian power structure escapes the judgment. Declares the King, whose name is YHWH of hosts — the royal divine title grounds the judgment's authority in YHWH's sovereign identity.
58: The fall of Babylon's broad walls — the wall of Babylon shall be thoroughly breached; her high gates will be set on fire — addresses one of the most famous features of ancient Babylon. The Greek historian Herodotus described Babylon's walls as 85 feet thick and 335 feet high (likely exaggerated but reflecting genuine impressiveness); the inner city wall is archaeologically confirmed as substantial. The outer city gate (the Ishtar Gate, now in the Pergamon Museum, Berlin) was indeed burned in later periods. The closing phrase — the peoples labor for nothing and the nations weary themselves only for fire — echoes Habakkuk 2:13.
59: The historical narrative of the scroll given to Seraiah son of Neriah — Baruch's brother, an official accompanying Zedekiah to Babylon in his fourth year (594 BC, likely a submissive diplomatic visit) — introduces the final sign-act of the book. Seraiah's title sar menuḥah (quartermaster, literally "chief of the resting-place") identified him as the official responsible for securing lodgings during travel. Having Baruch's brother carry the Babylon-doom scroll to Babylon itself is a deliberate literary and theological act.
60: Jeremiah wrote in a scroll all the disaster that would come upon Babylon, all these words written about Babylon — a written scroll of the oracle was the standard method of prophetic preservation and commissioning. The scroll functions as the prophetic word made material: it carries the pronouncement from the prophet's mouth to the condemned location. The written word of judgment, delivered to Babylon by Babylon's own official, encapsulates the prophetic theology of the word as an active agent that accomplishes YHWH's purpose.
61: The instruction to Seraiah — when you come to Babylon, see that you read all these words — makes the reading of the scroll a prophetic act performed at the location being judged. The public reading of the judgment oracle at the condemned site is a form of ritual enactment: the words are released at the place where they will be fulfilled. The subsequent sign-act (throwing the weighted scroll into the Euphrates) completes the ritual — the words are both spoken at Babylon and consigned to the river that will bring her destruction.
62: The prayer to be spoken after reading — YHWH, you yourself spoke against this place to cut it off, that none will live in it, neither human nor animal, and that it will be desolate forever — is a covenant prayer that invokes YHWH's spoken word as the basis for the request. The prayer does not ask for something new; it asks YHWH to fulfill what he has already spoken. This form of prayer — reminding YHWH of his own promises/declarations and asking for their fulfillment — appears throughout the Psalms (Psalms 119:49) and is the theological basis of prophetic intercession.
63: The command — when you finish reading this scroll, tie a stone to it and throw it into the middle of the Euphrates — creates a sign-act with transparent symbolism. The stone-weighted scroll sinking to the bottom of the Euphrates enacts Babylon's fate: thus will Babylon sink and not rise. The Euphrates was Babylon's life-source (water, transport, commerce) and its boundary; throwing the doom-scroll into the river releases the judgment into the very artery of Babylonian civilization. The sign-act technique transforms a prophetic word into a performed ritual of inevitable fulfillment.
64: The declaration — thus will Babylon sink and not rise, on account of the disaster I am bringing upon her — is both the spoken interpretation of the sign-act and the closing oracle of the entire Babylon oracle collection. The appended note thus far the words of Jeremiah (ʿad-hennah divrei Yirmeyahu) closes the prophetic corpus at this climactic sign-act, before the historical appendix of chapter 52. The sinking scroll is the last image: prophecy executed at the river, sinking into the waters of the condemned empire, prophesying its irreversible fall.
